title: Inspection And Metrology Catching Up For High-Density Fan-Out Panel Packaging
gist: AI/HPC demand is pushing advanced packaging from silicon-interposer wafers (capped near 100x100mm) to fan-out panels up to 600x600mm, but high-density fan-out breaks legacy PCB-grade inspection: sub-2um RDL, sub-20um bump pitch, 9-layer via stacks, and panel warpage are forcing a costly retooling of optical AOI/metrology and opening the door to X-ray, IR, and ML-driven adaptive lithography.

insights.claim: Silicon interposer-based packages are effectively capped at roughly 100x100mm before yield, lithography, warpage, and cost become unreasonable, forcing AI/HPC makers toward panel-level packaging as compute demand hits an area limit.
insights.speaker: Anne Meixner
insights.so_what: The 100mm interposer ceiling is the structural reason PLP capex is coming; watch panel-tool and materials suppliers as the next AI-packaging spend wave.
insights.quote: Silicon interposer-based packages are generally limited to 100 x 100mm, the largest size at which yield, lithography, warpage, and cost are reasonable.
insights.locator: Once primarily relegated to small chips

insights.claim: Panel-level packaging form factors will range from 310x310mm to 600x600mm, versus wafer-based limits, dramatically enlarging the substrate area that inspection and metrology tools must cover.
insights.speaker: Anne Meixner
insights.so_what: A ~600mm panel is ~36x the area of a 100mm interposer, reshaping throughput and tool-stage economics for equipment vendors.
insights.quote: with panel-level packaging, form factors will range from 310 x 310 mm to 600×600 mm
insights.locator: Once primarily relegated to small chips

insights.claim: TSMC has positioned panel-level packaging as a complement to CoWoS for some very large packages rather than a replacement, signaling coexistence of interposer and panel roadmaps at the leading foundry.
insights.speaker: Anne Meixner
insights.so_what: PLP is additive TAM, not a substitution threat to CoWoS revenue; both roadmaps can grow simultaneously.
insights.quote: which TSMC has said will complement CoWoS, not replace it, in some very large packages
insights.locator: Once primarily relegated to small chips

insights.claim: Package sizes for AI/HPC devices are already growing to 80x80mm and beyond as the need for compute in a single package outstrips available area.
insights.speaker: Anne Meixner
insights.so_what: Larger single-package die budgets favor multi-chiplet integration and drive the packaging-tool upgrade cycle.
insights.quote: panel-level packaging has become increasingly attractive for AI/HPC device makers as package size grows to 80 x 80mm and beyond
insights.locator: Once primarily relegated to small chips

insights.claim: Supporting high-density fan-out raises RDL layer count from 3 to 9 while trace and bump pitches shrink from 20 microns to 5 microns, with industry experts targeting 2-micron pitch within the next five years.
insights.speaker: Anne Meixner
insights.so_what: Each shrink step multiplies required inspection resolution and layer-count inspection steps; a durable tailwind for metrology tool intensity per panel.
insights.quote: layer count goes from 3 to 9, and trace and bump pitches decrease from 20 to 5 microns. In addition, several industry experts said that manufacturers are targeting 2 micron pitches within the next 5 years.
insights.locator: Fan-out panel-level packaging typically uses a chip-last

insights.claim: HDFO panels demand wafer/fab-class specs including sub-2um RDL line/space, sub-20um bump pitch, sub-micron overlay, hybrid-bond-ready surfaces, transparent-substrate handling, and through-glass via characterization on formats historically inspected at PCB-grade sensitivity.
insights.speaker: Arun Aiyer
insights.so_what: The spec jump from PCB-grade to fab-grade is the core disruption; incumbents with wafer-class optics have an adaptation edge over PCB-AOI vendors.
insights.quote: This migration brings wafer/fab class requirements onto formats that were historically inspected with PCB-grade sensitivity
insights.locator: Still, the shift to HDFO panels does demand more

insights.claim: Fan-out panel packaging uses a chip-last process, so committing an expensive known-good die onto an interposer or substrate requires proving the panel is defect-free first, elevating pre-bond inspection to a yield-protection gate.
insights.speaker: Monita Pau
insights.so_what: Chip-last economics make inspection a direct scrap-prevention lever protecting high-value HBM/TPU chiplets; inspection value scales with chiplet BOM cost.
insights.quote: You better make sure that these are defect-free before you commit a very expensive KGD onto the package.
insights.locator: For panel-level packaging, the first thing

insights.claim: Panel manufacturing feature sizes are converging toward front-end wafer-processing requirements, which fundamentally increases the amount of inspection required per panel.
insights.speaker: Monita Pau
insights.so_what: Inspection intensity per unit area rises toward wafer-fab levels, expanding served market for wafer-grade metrology players in packaging.
insights.quote: The smallest feature size becomes more like the front-end (i.e., wafer processing). This drives more inspection.
insights.locator: For panel-level packaging, the first thing

themes.theme: Resolution vs throughput vs data deluge

insights.claim: Halving optical resolution with the same camera cuts inspection speed roughly fourfold, so moving RDL inspection from 5-micron to 1-micron lines imposes a severe throughput penalty on panel scanning.
insights.speaker: JD Shin
insights.so_what: The quadratic speed hit means finer nodes require faster scanning hardware or new architectures; a moat for vendors who solve the sensitivity/throughput curve.
insights.quote: If you halve the resolution with the same type of camera, your inspection speed will be slower by four times.
insights.locator: Miniaturization stresses the optical systems

insights.claim: Inspecting a 600mm panel at 1-micron resolution generates on the order of 10^12 pixels per layer, turning panel inspection into a data-management and compute challenge on top of an optics challenge.
insights.speaker: Anne Meixner
insights.so_what: Terapixel-per-layer volumes make compute, storage, and ML pipelines part of the metrology BOM; favors vendors with data/software stacks, not just optics.
insights.quote: the inspection of a 600 mm panel with 1 micron resolution results in ~1012 pixels per layer
insights.locator: This increase in throughput time

insights.claim: Panel inspection faces an inescapable sensitivity-versus-throughput tradeoff where the binding constraint is never missing killer defects, so tools must balance rather than maximize either axis.
insights.speaker: Monita Pau
insights.so_what: Whoever shifts the sensitivity/throughput Pareto frontier wins tool selection; this is the core competitive axis in panel inspection.
insights.quote: Either you have high sensitivity and lower throughput, or you have high throughput but lower sensitivity.
insights.locator: It's always sensitivity versus throughput

insights.claim: As RDL stacks grow to 9 layers the via stack-up deepens while via area shrinks, and AOI cannot verify inter-layer via stacking, so today electrical test screens via defects despite providing late, less-actionable data.
insights.speaker: Kyoung Rok Park
insights.so_what: A capability gap in early via inspection creates an opening for X-ray and infrared modalities to displace or supplement electrical test.
insights.quote: Even if we perform inspection at every single layer with AOI, how do you know about the stacking?
insights.locator: The RDL layers are becoming complicated

insights.claim: The limits of optical AOI on via stacking and buried defects are opening the door to infrared and X-ray inspection modalities for earlier, more timely defect detection in panel packaging.
insights.speaker: Anne Meixner
insights.so_what: Watch X-ray and IR inspection suppliers as adjacent beneficiaries as HDFO via stacks outrun optical inspection.
insights.quote: That, in turn, opens the door for other modalities such as infrared and X-ray.
insights.locator: Park noted that today electrical test

insights.claim: Shrinking bump/micropillar pitch and rising aspect ratios make traditional 2D inspection insufficient, forcing multi-camera-angle 3D imaging to capture bump height and volume because bump quality directly drives bonding yield.
insights.speaker: JD Shin
insights.so_what: 3D metrology becomes mandatory rather than optional at fine pitch, expanding the addressable market for 3D-capable inspection vendors like Koh Young.
insights.quote: Three-dimensional measurement/inspection becomes more critical than in the past when you had bigger bump sizes.
insights.locator: As bumps/micropillars' pitch decreases

insights.claim: Bump/micropillar planarity requirements stay fixed even as pillar heights grow, making coplanarity harder to hit; measuring resist thickness and uniformity at the lithography stage enables rework, unlike post-formation coplanarity metrology.
insights.speaker: Chris Claypool
insights.so_what: Shifting metrology upstream to the resist stage enables rework and yield recovery, a differentiated value proposition versus post-process inspection.
insights.quote: You lose yield if bumps or micropillars are not coplanar.
insights.locator: In the bumps/micropillar lithography process

insights.claim: Conventional ellipsometry or reflectometry cannot measure films thicker than about 30 microns, but Bruker's optical design measures up to 2,000 microns of dielectric at nanometer resolution, a spec unnecessary for front-end but critical for bump/pillar planarity.
insights.speaker: Chris Claypool
insights.so_what: Thick-film-at-nanometer-resolution capability is a concrete Bruker differentiator gating entry to packaging planarity metrology.
insights.quote: Normally with ellipsometry or reflectometry, you're not going to be able to measure anything thicker than 30 microns.
insights.locator: Bruker has an optical design

insights.claim: Bump/pillar dielectric thicknesses are rising rapidly across generations, from an original 50-100 micron range to 450 microns last generation and up to 650 microns in the next generation now in development.
insights.speaker: Chris Claypool
insights.so_what: A clear multi-generation thickness roadmap sets the metrology spec race; tools stuck at 30-micron limits get designed out.
insights.quote: the last generation was up to 450 microns, and the next generation we're working on now is going to be up to 650 microns
insights.locator: Bruker has an optical design

themes.theme: Warpage as the dominant panel obstacle

insights.claim: Warpage is one of the most critical panel-packaging challenges, causing handling instability, die shift, photolithography alignment errors, yield loss, reliability issues, and electrical performance degradation as process uniformity degrades over larger areas.
insights.speaker: Gabriela Pereira
insights.so_what: Warpage is the cross-cutting failure driver; solving it (handling, correction, metrology) is the gating problem for PLP adoption timelines.
insights.quote: Warpage control is one of the most critical challenges, creating handling instability, die shift, photolithography alignment errors, yield loss, reliability issues, and electrical performance degradation.
insights.locator: The biggest challenges are concentrated around scale

insights.claim: FOPLP shows significantly higher warpage than equivalent FOWLP because CTE-induced warpage scales with dimension, with differential thermal expansion between polymers, metals, and silicon inducing severe bow and die shift during curing and debonding.
insights.speaker: Arun Aiyer
insights.so_what: Warpage worsening with panel size is a physics headwind, not an engineering nuisance; it structurally favors larger investment in handling and correction.
insights.quote: FOPLP (fan-out panel-level packaging) shows significantly higher warpage than equivalent FOWLP (fan-out wafer-level packaging) because CTE-induced warpage scales with dimension.
insights.locator: The central challenge is that panel packaging

insights.claim: Finer optical resolution narrows focal depth, so even small warpage can blur the inspection image, coupling resolution gains to a warpage-sensitivity penalty.
insights.speaker: JD Shin
insights.so_what: Resolution and warpage tolerance trade off physically, meaning higher-res tools need better panel flattening or autofocus to be usable.
insights.quote: If you have small resolution, you have much narrower focal depths. So even with very small warpage, the image that you receive can be very blurry.
insights.locator: Another challenge is that if you have finer resolution

insights.claim: Panel handling to flatten warpage (vacuum suck-down) competes with throughput because optical working distance requires avoiding collisions, so vendors must either flatten the panel or achieve fast auto-focus with a reference base, or combine both.
insights.speaker: Samuel Lesko
insights.so_what: Panel flattening plus fast autofocus is a design fork that differentiates tool architectures and depends on substrate material choice.
insights.quote: So either you find a way to suck down the panel, or you find a way to get fast auto-focus. Or, you combine both techniques.
insights.locator: The first challenge you face is the warpage

insights.claim: KLA and Onto Innovation leverage cross-platform architectures adaptable to PLP and advanced IC substrates with minimal modification, while some vendors build PLP-dedicated tools, framing an adapt-existing vs. purpose-build competitive split.
insights.speaker: Gabriela Pereira
insights.so_what: Incumbents KLA and Onto get a capital-efficiency edge by reusing platforms; dedicated-tool startups must prove a differentiated payback.
insights.quote: Leading suppliers such as KLA and Onto Innovation leverage cross-platform architectures that can be adapted to PLP and advanced IC substrates with minimal modifications, while some vendors have developed PLP-dedicated tools.
insights.locator: Existing advanced packaging metrology and inspection tool platforms

insights.claim: Panel packaging combines wafer-class feature sizes with PCB-class mechanical instability, meaning critical defects are no longer purely visual and a panel can pass conventional AOI yet fail because local height, overlay, or stress signatures were not measured in context.
insights.speaker: Arun Aiyer
insights.so_what: Pass/fail AOI is structurally blind to distortion-coupled failures, implying a fundamental tool-architecture gap that current AOI vendors have not closed.
insights.quote: A panel can pass conventional AOI and still fail because local height, overlay, or stress signatures were not measured in context.
insights.locator: The central challenge is that panel packaging combines

insights.claim: Inspection is shifting from a pass/fail gate to the data engine for adaptive lithography, where offline die-shift metrology feeds ML algorithms driving global, zonal, per-die, or site-by-site overlay correction.
insights.speaker: Arun Aiyer
insights.so_what: The value migrates from optics to closed-loop metrology-plus-ML software; watch for platforms that couple die-shift data to litho correction.
insights.quote: It is becoming the data engine for adaptive lithography, where offline die-shift metrology feeds ML algorithms that drive global, zonal, per-die, or site-by-site overlay correction.
insights.locator: Panel-level packaging inspection and metrology is at an inflection point

insights.claim: The industry still lacks a single high-throughput, closed-loop, panel-native platform that can correlate visual defects, dimensional errors, and process-induced deformation in one coordinate frame across a full panel up to roughly 600x600mm.
insights.speaker: Arun Aiyer
insights.so_what: An unmet whitespace for a unified panel-native platform signals a greenfield opportunity for whichever vendor closes it first.
insights.quote: the industry lacks a single high-throughput, closed-loop, panel-native platform that can correlate visual defects, dimensional errors, and process-induced deformation in one coordinate frame across the full panel
insights.locator: Panel-level packaging inspection and metrology is at an inflection point

insights.claim: Panel size standardization, warpage control, and equipment readiness remain unresolved, keeping inspection and metrology from moving cleanly from a discrete quality gate to a core enabler of manufacturability.
insights.speaker: Arun Aiyer
insights.so_what: Unresolved panel-size standards are a gating risk on PLP ramp timing; convergence is a leading indicator to watch before capex accelerates.
insights.quote: panel-size standardization, warpage control, and equipment readiness remain unresolved
insights.locator: Still, the shift to HDFO panels does demand more
